reviews.review_text: I played... my son got old enough to sit with me and explore... then old enough to take the controller and go on his own adventures. Incredible game, we leave it on. Literally a lifetime worth of content. Get in the mix with a battle if youre feeling frisky, explore the deepest oceans, build the highest bases, or be lazy and look for lush systems with unique environments. I'm writing this on the 10th anniversary update as it downloads a ton of new (and always free!) content. Never wrote a review before, probably wont again.

Awesome graphics, awesome gameplay, fine optimisation, and a ton of content. It may seem as walking simulator, or  too much grind, but it's less than 5% of the game. The longer you play, the less you need to walk or grind for materials, so it doesn't get repetetive as some might say. Would reccomend if you like exploration and calm gameplay. However, the game offers survival and permadeath mode (or even custom difficulty), which allows for much more challenging gameplay than usually.

No Man's Sky is awesome until, quite suddenly, it is not.  You begin by awakening on an unknown planet with a likely hostile environment (maybe), and you'll have to repair your equipment and survive using only what is available around you.  Over a few hours, you'll discover new elements and new recipes to build a base and repair your ship while discovering more about the game's world.  

Over a few more hours, No Man's Sky abandons the Open World Survival Model and morphs into an exploration game.  You'll have to find and explore many different planets and biomes to discover the big mystery behind who you are and why you are here, as well as learn the languages of the many (three) races that populate the galaxy and have surprisingly little to say or offer.  You will not need to survive on any of these planets.  We are done with that now.  

Of course, that doesn't mean you're scot-free.  No, there is a thriving economy and you can learn to master it.  Make billions by discovering and running trade routes between systems to maximize profit.  Learn the secrets of the trade radar.  Or you can discover recipes to create your own products and run factories to also make billions of credits.  Billions!  You'll need to upgrade your ship's warp drive to get to the planets with the most valuable ore and flora, of course, so get ready to spend!  Until you've bought everything.  Then you'll never need money ever again. Ever.  

Worried about this new venture cutting into your exploration time?  Don't be.  Exploring is completely unnecessary and can be fully wrapped up in less than 20 hours along side the main quest, as planets begin to repeat themselves down to even minor details very quickly.

There is a fantastic main story that deals in the themes of life, free will, the nature of existence.  Enjoy it, because once it is over, it is never addressed again outside of another repeatable questline masquerading as content.  Content that adds nothing but repeats of the same content.  This is by design.  Content repeating itself into infinity with little to no change as an alternative to death is a central theme of No Man's Sky.

There are side stories delivered as rewards for finding and solving a puzzle in various buildings across the universe.  These are stories of other "travellers" like yourself, and how their tales unfolded.  Or stories of the Atlas and the lore behind the sentinels.  These stories, like everything else in No Man's Sky, run out quickly and become replaced with flavorless placeholder text.

No Man's Sky is a massive shared universe where all players on all platforms exist having exactly the same experience as you at the same time!  Isn't that exciting?  No Man's Sky as an MMORPG?!?  No.  It is most certainly not that.  Outside of a single central area, you will not encounter other players nor encounter any content requiring the help of other players.  The economy or encounters of the game are not affected by these other players, and if you chose, you'd never have to see another player to fully appreciate No Man's Sky.  

Hello Games adds more content like this all the time.  The number of free updates that have added new features to No Man's Sky for is unrivaled.  New ships!  Large mobile bases called freighters that further eliminate the Survival aspects of No Man's Sky.  New exotic ship types to add to your collection, none of which add anything to your in game life.  Combat and piracy, with absolutely zero consequences at all!  Max out your favorite ship, hire a squadron, and prepare for the most mediocre and bland space combat ever! Pew pew pew.  Or not.  Hello Games clearly couldn't care less.

Just in the last 6 months, Hello Games has added two new full features.  Garbage collection!  New piles of trash have shown up all over the universe.  Use your new lifter gun to move the trash into your truck, and drive real slow so it doesn't all fall out.  If it does, use your lifter gun to put it in the truck.  Deliver the trash to the trash people.  You will not be using the new lifter gun in any other meaningful way.  Also some kind of Pokemon thing that, frankly, I can't even be bothered to make of.

You should play No Man's Sky.  It's full of big ideas and beautiful planets.  Just be sure to leave before the magic wears thin.

I bounced off this game a couple of times but finally got into it a couple of months ago. It's true that it is broad but shallow in many ways - but this is still some of the most immersive fun I've had in VR. There is something supremely mellow about hanging out in space and being able to walk on a planet's surface, jump abroad your spaceship, fly off into orbit, hyperdrive to the nearest space station, land, go to the portal and teleport all the way to your base. And NMS does some pretty cool things in terms of VR user interface and controls (even if some other things could be improved a lot, such as the inventory system).

When this game was announced I was astonished by all the things it promised to deliver, then the launch came. Deciding whether to buy this game or not I decided otherwise. Even tho the game astonished me the delivered product was far from satisfactory. Few years went by and I heard about No Mans Sky again, now looking even better than what I originally expected. I bought game pass to try it out and spent around 200h playing. The game has done way more than redeem its sins. It became one of the greatest games ever made and developers really made effort to uphold their promises to 200%. Incredible world, boundless options and hours on hours of gameplay that just sucks you in. Year after and I also decided to buy the game on steam. Hands down the best purchase I have ever made on Steam. I cant recommend this game enough, worth every penny. Now Im just waiting for Light no Fire, if the developers learnt their lesson for the launch of NMS then we are in for an incredible journey and I just cant wait for it.

Sadly for all of the years of development and work, the game is still boring. Tons of updates brought a lot of new and different things to do, but every old and new mechanic is simple and lacks progression. The worst part is that with the size of the galaxy - your experience, except visually, does not change. If you fought sentinels on this planet it will be same experience 100 star systems away. Same old few missions, same old buildings on different edges of a galaxy. It's dull in it's samyness. 
Exploration doesn't matter, mechanically planets have same missions, same underlying structure, you don't need to prepare or care on what planet you are, except what resources you need. And very fast it becomes logistics and resource management game instead of a exploration game. Which wouldn't be a problem if resource management and logistics wouldn't be so shallow and boring.
Trade doesn't matter, interaction between factions is none existent and it makes every star system samish and non-important. Space fights are dull. What type of space ship you fly doesn't really matter, partly because of uninspired mechanics, partly because there is no real difference between said space ships. Of course, except visually and how much stuff it can carry.
In the end you're just watching how much more stuff you have, more currencies, more bases, more pretty ships, more resources, but in the end you're doing the same thing that you did in the star system you spawned. And if you don't enjoy this bare bones game loop, it depressingly wouldn't change in 100s of star systems.
I would like to love this game, but sadly after all the years of development, it's - ironically, for all of it's visual diversity, bare bones, knee deep and generic in gameplay.

It's one hundred percent worth it. Although one gripe is the planets don't orbit anything they just stay still in the system. So Hello Games if you are reading this please create actual star systems.

If you are on the fence just buy it it'll give you hours of fun.

This is honestly one of the best sci-fi and space-themed games I've ever played ( after Elite Dangerous ofc). I literally fell in love with this game.

Ever since I was a kid, I've been fascinated by space. I've spent countless hours reading about the universe, learning about planets, stars, black holes, and the possibility of extraterrestrial life. The idea that there could be something beyond our world has always captured my imagination, and that's one of the reasons why No Man's Sky feels so special to me.

The sense of exploration in this game is incredible. Landing on completely unknown planets, discovering strange creatures, traveling through vast star systems, and simply looking at the sky from an alien world never gets old. It gives me the feeling of being part of a huge, living universe where there's always something new waiting to be found.

What I love most is how the game lets you create your own adventure. Sometimes I spend hours exploring planets, sometimes building bases, and other times just flying through space and enjoying the atmosphere. The freedom it offers is something I rarely find in other games.

As someone who has always dreamed about space exploration and the possibility of life beyond Earth, No Man's Sky feels like a game made for people like me. It's not just a game about survival or progression it's a game that lets you experience the wonder of the unknown. For me that's what makes it one of the greatest space games ever made.

I really, really want to like this game, but it just makes me feel horrible. Playing this feels like a bad fever dream, invoking a similar feeling to looking at AI art or playing ♥♥♥♥♥♥ mobile games. Hours lost looking for something, anything of substance. Credit where credit is due, it's interesting that the developers chose a narrative about a fake, digital universe, and the psychological consequences for it's inhabitants, because much like the NPCs of No Man's Sky, I'm essentially constantly coping about the pervasive lack of meaning while playing.

Its ability to invoke this emotion is due to a systematic lack of depth and difficulty that is everywhere in this game, even on survival. Every planet has different window dressings, but the gameplay is the same on every one of them. It's great for the first system or two, then the fatigue sets in. You'd think an intergalactic explorer would have no dearth of problems to solve. but in NMS it's the same ♥♥♥♥ over and over. 

Differing atmospheric compositions, survival aspects, weather, environmental hazards, hostile lifeforms - everything gets reduced to charging a battery, and making sure you bring enough batteries. You figure out the battery part pretty quickly, and then the game becomes trivially easy because you can buy them in bulk from the nearest space station. Good survival games require hunting for food, finding and mapping resources like water sources and craftables, managing exposure or crafting things like specific clothing or shelters, treating ailments or injuries with specific cures. In NMS, it is all battery charging for your suit.

Animal behavior? Simplified to 2 main things - walk at you to bite you, or walk away from you. Charges, tail swipes, grabs, envenomation, projectiles, parasitic behavior, paralysis, traps - none of that. 254 hours and I still haven't gotten eaten by anything. No getting hunted down by a pack of something nasty, no getting thrown about by an alien gorilla, no sarlacc pit moment, nothing interesting or fun at all. There is giant worms in the game that physically could swallow your ship, but they're just window dressing, they ignore you and don't actually do anything. Most animals also pop in and out at about 30m with no persistence whatsoever, completely ruining immersion. Just no effort at all at anything other than visuals.

Any combat depth (which is still not much) was given to the sentinels instead. Probably the worst conceived idea for an exploration sandbox game I've ever seen, essentially GTA cops except on some planets they will attack you without provocation. You don't get anything for fighting them. As the player, the one thing that offers a modicum of combat intrigue, you are completely incentivized to avoid and never fight - why??? One thing every single NMS player will do is groan when they see an aggressive sentinel planet.

Space dogfights are okay. Not great. Just okay - at least they are a little profitable.

Economy and progression? I hope you had fun grinding vortex cubes for 10 hours, because this game will throw end game freighters worth 10's of millions at you for free. Pay credits to make your battery bigger or your inventory larger to collect more BS. A few hours in collecting storm crystals and you basically have little need for credits anymore.

On top of all of these core issues, the devs have slapped countless underbaked systems - a broken unfun settlement system, a shallow basebuilding system that you will ignore in favor of freighters/corvettes, word collectathon, derelict freighters in space that are somewhat interesting but that you never see, vehicles that you won't use until the very end, fishing minigame I never touched, ♥♥♥♥ it pokemon I guess, the list goes on and on. Most of it just bloats the game and doesn't make it any more fun to play. Devs are basically headless chickens but they get endlessly glazed by the gaming community for some reason.

Game sux don't buy it.
probably a good game for base building etc or something like Minecraft.  But has boring spaceship fights, same buildings in all planets I visited, to many items, constantly fueling.

As far as the most recent expedition(Swarm) goes it is a massive waste of time. They start you with a fake personality quiz to trick you into thinking your choices matter when in reality you're randomly assigned to a faction. They want to focus on the multiplayer aspects of the game, but then they make it where playing co-op with your friend isn't viable because you're in competition against each other.

On top of that they were to stupid to make the areas immediately around certain objectives no base building zones so people can just grief by building their bases in the way after doing things themselves.
this game has come a long way. WARNING it is easy to get distracted and find yourself just having fun.

It's just boring game, it is as wide as an ocean and as shallow as a puddle. There's no story, little to do. Yeah I get it's an "exploration game" but the planets, flora and fauna - all look exactly alike. You're just exploring the same few planets over and over again with no reason.

The core gameplay is FINE if you are really into that: exploration. But don't expect depth in any of the other content the game offers, it is all extremely shallow and serves only as dressing for the main "thing" of the game.

The biggest issue, BY FAR, of this game, is the baffling design choices that you encounter constantly. Things that make you tab out and google to make sure you're not stupid and just missing something, just to find out that no, it's by design, and people have been complaining about it since the game's release.
Some quick ones that are noticeable within 30 minutes of playing; 
No "save and quit", you have to find a save point, or enter/leave your ship, and then alt-f4 as your only means of leaving the game.

The tutorial tells you to go somewhere, and puts a visible marker for you to go to. The marker however then dissapears, and the tool you can build to locate things, such as that marker you need to find, is not unlocked yet, so you better have remembered!

Speaking of the tutorial, you get a little text box at the bottom of your screen that tells you the next steps that you have to work for, which is nice. HOWEVER, this box constantly switches from the objective you're on (eg: "craft a wire to repair the ship"), to useless text akin to "mine iron ore for iron :)", and the only way to get the useful guide/objective back up is to wait for the chaff to cycle through, like an ad.

Pressing escape will, unintuitively, not close whatever menu you're inside of; it will open a separate compendium-style menu. This means that constantly while playing you will be opening and closing 3-5 different menus by accident for something that should have taken one key press.

Some quests require you to craft things to repair machines/etc, but they won't just let you craft it in your inventory, where the thing you need to repair is; you have to switch to another tab in your inventory, craft the component you need (hope you remembered what it was!), and then go back to the original tab to drag it into the slot. This is small, but it happens so often, and just shows what kind of ride you're in for with the UI/UX in the game.

This is barely the tip of the iceberg, but the game has been out for YEARS, and I am finding complaints about these exact things back from release, which can only mean that they are known, and are design choices, despite them resulting in an extremely frustrating experience.

If you really like exploring, it's fine, but even then the game is, in my opion, ugly as sin on top of everything else.

I've been gaming for over 30 years.
This is what modern game development should be.
Don't ever change Hello Games. We love you.

No mans sky became my mans sky.

After so many updates the game is worth it!

8/10 - Great game. Story still kinda sucks, but the gameplay is fun.

I played no man's sky on release day on ps4. I played it for longer than most people should. I loved my time with it... i'm a big ol sci fi fan so its not hard for a space game to grab my attention. 

I'm going to recommend this game to people that haven't played it like me, cause you don't know. 

Now here is my critique... The first SEVERAL hours of this game are LITERALLY unchanged from its initial release. Nothing feels different, you are mining grabbing stuff, and just spend your first few hours doing the same things you did ON DAY 1 with a few extra steps and things you need to upgrade. Which for me? was extremely off putting... 

Then I got into the settlement stuff, started having fun with that, and then you have to wait 20/40/50/2 hours to complete a building. I'm sure the intent is for you to do things in the mean time, but I don't like it. Why can't i upgrade multiple buildings? its not like it takes citizens to upgrade them? if it did i would prioritize getting citizens but it doesn't... So why can't i upgrade multiple things at once?

this is the part where i realized this game is okay with wasting your time. I jumped on to upgrade a building that would take 2 hours to finish upgrading. I then went to work out, and make food, and came back to realize what i had always feared but i had just forgotten to do. If you don't jump into your ship and jump out to save that EXTREMELY important interaction/upgrade. Guess what? it never happened. I'm at the point in time in my life where I dont like games that waste my time, or have mechanics where i have to wait, especially if it doesn't make sense. 

why can't i upgrade my overseer building and the more i upgrade that the more upgrades i can have active at a single time?

why can't i see the materials i need so i can go gather them while its upgrading? it just says "you have an active upgrade happening on another building" come on bro... that's just cheeks. 

maybe its totally my fault and I should haven't messed with settlements so early? then maybe i shouldn't be allowed to interact with one until i've done certain milestones, but then that sucks too. I don't know. Maybe i'm just crazy and nobody else feels this way, which is possible.

Once again though, i have been enjoying the game, but i do feel like there are elements of this game that just want to waste my time, and i'm not about that, and i don't know if i will continue to play more of this because of that. i'm gonna thumb this up cause i think my problem just may be a ME thing, and not something everyone else will have, and others may enjoy it. there also isn't a neutral recommendation, if there is i am dumb and don't see it LMAO
for a 10 year old game, why do i need google, to play with friends? why is multiplayer so hard to execute?
A great Exploration, leisure and inner thinking game,

I bought this on release on PlayStation 4 and i loved it. Why? Because i didn't watch the trailer. I didn't have any expectations. I just bought a game i thought looked cool and played it. Never looked back. I still have this on my PS4 as well as my PS5. Bought it on Steam cause why the hell not right? They've worked on this game since launch to make it better, and i think they've done exactly that. Hello Games never gave up on this, and i don't think they ever will. I will continue to play NMS, as well as eagerly awaiting for Light No Fire to release. 

Thank You Hello Games, for all of your dedication to make No Man's Sky better.
The game is great, whole lot of gameplay and updates.

I want to like this game and return to it for a few hours each year since launch, but it's still very much just the exact same thing ad infinitum, but I guess now they've added pokemon or something.

I'd love to explore the universe but this game gives you no reason to do so. You can travel a million lightyears and forget that you did. Mine this, now upgrade your laser so that you can mine other stuff, that and now you can upgrade your ship so that you can upgrade your warp drive so that you can warp further, but warping further doesn't do anything.

I wanted to like this game, but it wasn't for me.

My first impression was that the tutorial dragged on far too long. Nearly 80 minutes into the game, I was still being sent on resource-gathering errands and building a base instead of feeling like I was exploring the universe. A poor starting planet didn't help, as much of my time was spent walking around looking for basic materials rather than doing anything exciting.

The controls and camera also felt awkward to me, and I never reached a point where movement or exploration felt comfortable. While I understand the game opens up later, I don't think a player should have to spend several hours waiting for the fun to begin.

Not a bad game—just one whose opening hours failed to hook me.

This is my third copy of this game. There are no regrets... anymore.
When the game came out, there was not enough substance to it. but now you probably struggle to really run out of things to do. The only thing I don't like is the camera, I prefered the old one.

As a side note, the fact that the player base has continued to this point is a testament to the dedication of the team. Imagine MS or Sony running this show behind the scenes, it would have just been deleted at launch.

What keeps pulling me back is the atmosphere and sense of discovery. Flying seamlessly from orbit to planet surface still hits every time, and the game constantly rewards curiosity instead of forcing a checklist. It’s one of the few games where I can genuinely relax, explore, build, survive, or just disappear into the universe for a few hours.
Still no normal sync between players constructions after all these years. Coop building is literally impossible.

I finally purchase this game. I normally dont spend much time playing games, even that I love than. Normally are short session of maximum 2 hours. Maybe some quests, runs or something and stop for the day. But this game is different. From the begining, the fact that is procedural generated, makes me very intrested. I can explore for hours, and still have things to do. Is relaxing, compared with many other online games, and the fact you have all the space to just relax, is one of the best parts. I already collected many stories from the two days I played.

i realize it's already 906 hours of gametime and i have yet to leave a review. What can i say, i played this back then when it came out, wasn't part of the hype train, but i was disappointed. I know that it's probably a trivial footnote compared to the games history. But we have to acknowledge it, it was bad when it came out. We shouldn't forget that so that we can truly appreciate what it is now, a game that has surpassed the expectations a whole generation. it's is a living proof that with effort, dedication and passion, you can create something truly good. So cheers to hello Games and NMS. i am excited to support your future projects

Amazing game! Probably the best sci fi adventure game!
Full of content and things to do!.
Amazing cinematic graphics!
Lightweight and with good FPS on slower machines!

I cannot recommend this game enough
No other games offers so much for so little.
Updates after updates free of charge, no DLC's and no micro transactions

We all know the story of no man's sky at this point. I have real respect to the devs for continuing to put out new updates to this game year after year. Never once have they charged me for a dlc, battle pass, or some kind of cosmetic. Despite all this i dont really know if i can recommend no man's sky. 

as a teen i had put nearly 200 hours into this game and since then roughly 30 or so more when i come by to see whatever expansion catches my eye. its a game i want to like. its just really, really, really boring. looking at it now i can imagine that the first time you play it this game is really cool. theres lots of things to do, lots of things to see, but as you go around doing and seeing these thing they all start to blend together. youve seen one tropical planet youve seen them all. exploration on the whole is a bust, its the same buildings everywhere with a different coat of paint, the same sentinels, the same repeatable quests. 
the expeditions serve as a temporary distraction from this sameness, a nice little questline that shows you the in and out of a new update. my only real issue with them is that they are timegated. but even they dont solve the core issue. once you know how to easily get a good weapon, how to easily get currency, how to easily get a good ship then even the expeditions fall apart and your forced to look at what gameplay these tools exist in service of. 
grounded combat is bad, the camera is far to interested in the back of the players head and first person is quite ugly somehow. the sentinels pose no real threat to the player incredibly quickly, becoming more of a increasingly ignorable annoyance and a occasional good source of nanites. the small dissident sentinels have had a issue where their drops spawn to low to the ground and fall out of bounds for as long as i can remember them in the game. 
space combat is somehow worse. the game likes to have big showpiece fights in space, the raider dreadnaught for example, but the combat in those fights is holding s + m1. your ship will auto target anything in range, so you dont need to even look at the screen if you dont want to. it actively ruins the upgrade system for me because no matter how much i want to care about number go up tricknology the number will never meaningfully matter. 

all of this results in what is to me a sad game. a endless ocean with the depth of a puddle. its a shame that so much passion has resulted in such a empty game. i assume its a issue with technical debt, the game has been getting updated for a long while now and hello games were not a experienced dev team at the start. at this point i dont think any amount of updates will "fix" the core of this game. personally id like to see them start fresh and make something new. i think they could do a lot better this time

Maybe the brightest redemption story in modern gaming. It has been 10 years, and Hello Games is still supporting the game with new free content. 
I hope other developers will take an example and will not abandon their games even if the launch was bad in all directions.

No mans sky is a fantastic space game. It has so much to do and they keep adding massive expansions for free! I know it had a rocky start when it launched. But its so good now. No microtransactions, no pay to win. 10/10

There's still not much "game" here but the devs took the original steaming mess and turned it into something fun to ♥♥♥♥ around in, which is almost unique in the world of Video Game's, thus I respect.

Crazy how Hello Games, just turned everything around and has managed to fully support this game for 10 years plus without asking for a single cent from its player base, it really puts every other developer team into shame
3 hours in and I've already hit more progression than any other AAA game I've ever played. Great game and constantly on sale for a fantastic deal. you wont regret picking it up.

As a Star Citizen player, the flight is very simple (which I don't care) for but the sheer amount of things to do and free updates make No Mans Sky an all time favorite. I love this game and I think Hello Games needs to be put into some sort of developer hall of fame. They clearly love they're work, the game they've made, and the people who play it.

This game has come a long way. It's honestly impressive how the devs keep updating with big patches, that aren't paywalled behind DLC. I'm not super far in the game, but I can already see it's depth. Can't wait to ruin my marriage over this game.
Start bad, updates good, more updates better, many updates great

it says 37hrs, but my main save has over 250hrs not including expeditions. this game is worth every dollar. for 10 years they been tossing out free updates, and that is way more generous than they need to be. you guys already redeemed yourselves, but the more you pump out, the more of us will be here to support em. if you are reading this as of may 28th, make sure if you get the game to do the new swarm expedition. get lost in the game, have fun, do what you wish, but never forget sodium or oxygen, youll need a lot of it. 10/10 game and devs. kick ass into the future and we are ALL waiting for Light No Fire.

This is one of my favorite sandboxes in all my 20+ years gaming. If your the type of gamer that needs directional gameplay or a map full of cities and civilizations then this isn't for you. However, I would like to dispel the myth that theres nothing to do in No Mans Sky. You can build bases, corvettes and freighter bases if you enjoy building things. You can manage settlements and fleets of frigates if you like tycoon games. You can do guild missions, nexus missions and occasional expeditions if you enjoy those things. You can explore a seemingly infinite universe of various biomes, discover fauna and flora and ancient secrets if you simply like wandering with no aim. You can battle the fauna you catch in holographic arenas and hunt for rarer S class creatures across the 251 different galaxies. You can farm, trade, and provide delivery services. You can fight robots, alien creatures and other players when communities are at war. You can fish and explore deep seas with various underwater caves and creatures. I could go on, and on about all your options. Communities to join if you find yourself lonely, as well as playing in a group session up to four. No matter how into detail I go about your many, many options of playstyles and goals, I'll never name them all because Hello Games continues to drop free updates adding more and more content multiple times a year. And they're all FREE. I got this game at launch, played about 7 hours and didnt pick it back up until 3 years ago and I'm no where near close to the goals I've set for myself in my new home system. (I play almost everyday). If your wondering wether or not to get the game my advice is to try it out, get to the anamoly, explore until you get curious about everything in this review, finish the three main missions and look up community content. There are plenty of player communities that discover new things about the game everyday. Trust me, as long as this review is, I barely scratched the surface. Good luck Traveller!
run like potato but still run stable except sometime popup texture.

This is, in no doubt, one of, if not the BEST GAME EVER MADE! I cannot fit 10% of everything this game has to offer into one review, so I am just going to list some main features. You can, Explore completely randomized planets, explore a huge galaxy that no player has explored all of, build bases, drive vehicles, adopt alien pets, build huge ships, buy enormous vessels, have a starfighter battle, become an interstellar merchant, have animal battles, discover mysterious alien ruins, lounge with friends, barter items at the Interstellar huge space station that everyone goes to, learn an alien language, (Like literally, be able to speak it IRL), fight huge titan class monsters, change your character's species and clothing, and SO, MUCH, MORE! Now, I usually mention even the smallest issues with games that I review because I am critic, but there is literally NONE worth mentioning. 100% recommend to anyone who likes doing one of the things I just listed. CRITIC REVIEW: 9.9/10
This game has improved so much since its launch. It is genuinely amazing to see all the work the developers put into this game a decade after launch. 10/10

I really like this game.   Best space exploration and trading game. Includes some base building, but it’s not required. This game is freedom.

While it might be nice if you could have some AI guards or crewmen on your ship, that is the only thing that could be added that I can think of at this time.

Then a mod to replace the appearances of them to look like galactic imperial navy personnel.

Highly recommend ( if you are interested by exploration, some puzzles, and whatever else I have mentioned).
It has a nice feeling to it, kind of time consuming, but still enjoyable

Hands down one of the most fun space adventure games I've ever played, the seamless gameplay with almost no load times or in game interruptions immerses you in an endless open world universal experience with awesome space battles and even armada control, endless fun side quests and a completely unrestricted game play style which is honestly quite refreshing, instead of placing you in a sandbox and giving you options of what to do, No Mans sky places you in an open word  universe and says show us what you can do! space ranger or pirate, Miner, Explorer, super hero... Love it !!! the network play is just as seamless and meeting up with friends is super easy and interactive, the controls are intuitive and whilst the graphics are a bit cartoonish, it really gives the game a fun vibe, not entirely a simulation but a great blend between simulation mechanics and imagination, the original idea around the procedural generated worlds is pure genius and really sets it apart from anything I've ever played before, and not to mention the ship and base building mechanics, this game is so fun !!!  9/10

Rating: 7/10

Pretty good game, definitely the right game for exploration lovers. That being said, that's about it for the game. This game definitely feels like it came out a decade ago. The cycle of exploring, grinding, and moving on feels sort of empty. Combat is extremely boring and unpolished. The game doesn't really explain anything and not in a good way. The main storyline so far is convoluted and feels like there isn't much reason why my character should even be helping. 

Ship flying mechanics are...interesting. Also, fuel consumption is ridiculously high at first until you get the right upgrades. Fighting pirates occasionally was fun at first but quickly became boring. Otherwise, I think that ship combat needs an overhaul.

On to the three main races, which none of them feel real. They all feel bland and not easy to relate. The language system is interesting, but I can perfectly understand them on the Anomaly? Besides that, none of the three races feel all that different from each other. I understand the Gek are traders, Vykeen are warriors, and Korvax are scientists. However, that's about it for differences between the three races. There isn't really a complex system behind the standings with each race.

Lastly, and most importantly, there is so many bugs in the game that just make the game really unfun at certain points. If you can get the game while it's on sale, I'd recommend checking this game out if you're looking for an exploration game. Otherwise, I wouldn't recommend this game if you're looking for anything else.

You'd think after 10 years they would have at least made an attempt to get rid of the multitude of bugs that plague this game.
My chief concerns are the save game system and the ship load out. They MAKE you group tech to afford bonuses for that tech, but if you store the ship you have to regroup the the now fubar groupings?
The multiple crashes to desktop are always so much FUN! Like most software titles now days, instead of polishing their product and make it error free they just keep adding more and more content and let the bugs fester. They have been at this for 10 years - why are there so many glaring bugs?
awesome game escpecially if you like subnautica and minecraft

Keeps crashing. All. The. Time.

There has to be a memory leak somewhere. The game runs smoothly in all settings, until it doesn't. Crashes faster with higher settings, after few minutes. With lowest settings, I can play for maybe 15min, then boom. Crash. This game is almost 10y old, and it is as unstable as January 6th.

No Man’s Sky is one of those games that I really wanted to love more than I actually loved playing.

The atmosphere and sense of scale are genuinely impressive at first. Landing on new planets, flying into space seamlessly, and discovering strange creatures creates a strong initial feeling of wonder. But after several hours, I realized the core gameplay loop just wasn’t engaging enough for me.  Also, the creatures and planets all started to feel the same very quickly.

Most of my time felt spent:

mining resources
refueling systems
crafting materials
scanning things
flying to another marker
repeating the same cycle

For me, the game felt more wide than deep. The exploration started to lose its magic once I realized most interactions boiled down to variations of the same tasks with no real goal.

I also found the progression lacking tension or meaningful stakes. There wasn’t much unpredictability, danger, or emergent gameplay pushing me to adapt creatively. It felt more like a chill resource-management/exploration game than an actual survival adventure.  However the exploration didn't feel unique either.

If you’re looking for:

high tension
meaningful survival pressure
dynamic encounters
strategic depth
memorable emergent moments

this may not click for you the way it didn’t for me.

I was hoping this would be a fun survival/crafting game, but it was very disappointing.

The game is shallow as a puddle. You get introduced to new content that is easily finished in 30 minutes. There's no long term viability. It's basically just jump from planet to planet every 30 minutes to farm some resources and scan stuff. There's no depth and the game play loop is dull.

TLDR of my situation: I've got a base in the deep pumping out resources that I don't need since there's nothing left for me to do. I've got an S class pirate freighter. I've got and S class ship. Space combat isn't challenging just annoying when pirates try to jump me. My settlements are in the positive but nothing they produce I need. I've got 60 freighter upgrades left and 30 freighter storage upgrades I can't use due to limit and no tech left to unlock. My ship slots are maxed. I've got a staff, cyborg appearance and thousands of motes leftover to spend on nothing. I've got all the blueprints unlocked and the ones I want built in my ship. I've got around 126 million credits and around 400,000 nanites and nothing to spend it on. There's quicksilver but there's nothing for me to grind for.
In a good place. Lots of fun. Hardly any aggravations when playing, which is actually a high bar these days.

Having played 1.0 on the PS4 (remember that one?) and enjoying it, I feel it hard to express how breathtaking the progress and ongoing evolution of the game has been.

Initially ridiculed as "One Man's Lie", the game is now every single thing that was promised - and so much more.

It is and will likely be one of these gems I will come back to again abs again - not for the story, but to drink in moments like the dazzling vistas, endless sea of stars or deep hum of the freighter.

With the addition of the cruiser as a smaller "base", my space explorer fantasy is complete.

There is no way to disable auto-leveling of the ship, meaning if you want to do a roll to get a better view of the planet from the cockpit, you can't because the game re-angles your ship to the horizon automatically. Since I can't fully control my ship or disable aim assist, this is a deal breaker for me.

worst new user experience I've hit in decades. Absolutely unclear, no instruction on simple and utterly necessary stuff like what ****ing key does what. Looks good, and apparently vast, but unnecessarily and pointlessly frustrating (not due to the game but simply failing to actually introduce or show the minimum to even function). Nowhere that I could see was it possible to see what key does what (I assume it's somewhere, but gave up eventually), like to fly the freaking ship (i can roll and accelerate, ..and that's it? how dafuq do I aim this POS?). Sooo sooo soo many things are pointlessly frustrating just because they never introduce anything worth a damn...which in this day and age is just dumb. I found the UI moderately counterintuitive, but more than that just very badly documented/introduced.

To be fair, it looks nice-ish (sharp but simplified visuals in overly bright colors...kind of cartoony), runs well under linux with no trouble there. I like the idea, but the new player experience is just spectacularly poor.

The game is so immense and there is so much to do it never gets old.

One of the best video games I have ever played, but the PC version can be really frustrating at times. The exploration feels endless, and there’s always something new to discover. Flying through space, finding new planets, upgrading ships, building bases, and exploring strange worlds never really gets old. The atmosphere and soundtrack make the game feel relaxing and immersive, and it’s easy to lose hours playing without realizing it. The biggest problems for me were the performance issues, save file problems, and the unforgiving damage system for spaceships. The game didn’t crash much, but there were plenty of frame drops, stuttering, and moments where the performance felt inconsistent, especially during busy areas or long play sessions. The save issues were also stressful because it sometimes felt unreliable, making me worried about losing progress. Another frustrating part was how punishing spaceship damage can be. One bad mistake, rough landing, or fight can leave your ship heavily damaged, and repairing everything can become annoying fast, especially early in the game when resources are limited. Instead of feeling challenging, it sometimes just slowed the fun down.

After playimg this game for the past decade and witnessing its growt from 2016 onward, I can tell you that there is no shortage of things to do.  The tutorial sequence and main story are short enough for you to grasp the vital concepts of the game, then turms you loose to play the way you want to.  Do you want to build strong relationships with the races you encounter, or be the most feared pirate in the galaxy?  Or maybe spend your days fishing and cleaning up the world you call home, or be forever on the move -- charting the stars, their planets and the species that reside on them?  Maybe you'd rather task a crew of other players and go on various missions throught the many galaxies this experience has to offer?

If you played the game at launch and reasonably thought you made a mistake in purchasing the game, I would gladly tell you that the No Man's Sky of 2016 and the No Man's Sky of today are fundamentally different.  If you have yet to play the game, you will never regret te purchase!
awesome tecnical game, fun little sand box for friends, ships and just being a tool. lol

I've been playing No Man's Sky since day one, and I can honestly say I've never seen a game transform the way this one has.

What started as an ambitious space exploration game has become one of the greatest redemption stories in gaming history. Over the years, Hello Games has continued to deliver massive free updates that have expanded nearly every aspect of the experience: exploration, base building, freighters, settlements, expeditions, multiplayer, ship customization, planetary variety, and so much more.

The sense of scale remains incredible. Even after all these years, I still find myself landing on a new planet and discovering something I've never seen before. Whether I'm exploring distant star systems, building a new base, hunting for rare ships, or simply enjoying a sunset on an alien world, the game constantly reminds me why I keep coming back.

What impresses me most is the passion and dedication of the developers. Instead of abandoning the project, they kept improving it year after year, turning it into something truly special. Few studios have shown this level of commitment to their community.

No Man's Sky isn't just a good game anymore—it's a remarkable journey that continues to evolve. If you've ever dreamed of exploring an endless universe at your own pace, this is one of the best experiences available.

After playing since launch, I can confidently recommend it.

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

There's a rather large variation of gameplay mechanics and many different types of space exploration apparati. For the most part, they're all unlocked at the start after a few tutorial bits.

Speaking of the tutorial, the game's first save file can be a bit nasty depending on where you begin. It's all in good fun, though, so don't get frustrated. Once you leave your first planet, the universe pretty much opens up. I'm nowhere near the end of the first story and I've already gotten tons of storage full of materials and a pretty large arsenal of maxed-out ships and weaponry. I've discovered many solar systems in the universe, and from what I've found out, I haven't even scratched the surface of it... And this universe isn't the only one, either. It also has time travel of sorts, from what I understand.

For such a simple system, this game is absolutely massive, and I will absolutely recommend it. It's about the biggest sandbox you can find, and I've thus far not regretted my purchase. I play solo, but it would be good to have friends to cart around in a Corvette, too. Get it and have fun.
